title: Databases and Persistence
summary: Connect to databases, execute SQL queries, and use ORMs with connection pooling

sections.title: Databases are permanent, structured storage
sections.context: File-based storage (JSON, CSV) works for small projects but breaks down at scale. Databases provide concurrent access, data integrity, querying, and performance. SQLite is built into Python — perfect for local/single-user apps. PostgreSQL and MySQL are client-server databases for production web applications. Raw SQL gives you full control but requires manual connection management, escaping, and result parsing. SQLAlchemy is Python's most powerful database toolkit — it provides both raw SQL and an ORM (Object-Relational Mapper) that maps database tables to Python classes. An ORM lets you work with database records as if they're normal Python objects.

sections.title: SQLite with SQLAlchemy ORM
sections.content: Define models, create tables, insert and query records using an ORM
sections.code:
from sqlalchemy import create_engine, Column, Integer, String, Float
from sqlalchemy.orm import declarative_base, Session

# Create database engine — connects to a SQLite file
engine = create_engine("sqlite:///inventory.db", echo=True)  # echo logs SQL
# Declarative base — every model class inherits from this
Base = declarative_base()

class Product(Base):
    # Maps to the 'products' table in the database
    __tablename__ = "products"  # Table name in the database

    # Each Column maps to a database column with its type
    id = Column(Integer, primary_key=True)  # Auto-incrementing primary key
    name = Column(String(100), nullable=False)  # Name, max 100 chars, required
    price = Column(Float, nullable=False)  # Price as floating-point number
    quantity = Column(Integer, default=0)  # Stock count, defaults to 0

# Create the table in the database (run once)
Base.metadata.create_all(engine)  # Generates CREATE TABLE from model

# Insert a new product using the ORM
with Session(engine) as session:  # Session = database transaction
    # Create a Python object like any other class
    new_product = Product(name="Wireless Mouse", price=29.99, quantity=50)
    session.add(new_product)  # Stage the insert (not yet in database)
    session.commit()  # Actually execute the INSERT — persists to disk

    # Query: find all products under $30
    cheap_products = session.query(Product).filter(Product.price < 30).all()
    for p in cheap_products:
        print(f"{p.name}: ${p.price}")

sections.title: Connection pooling, migrations, and raw SQL
sections.context: Creating a new database connection for every request is expensive (handshake, authentication). Connection pooling (built into SQLAlchemy's create_engine) maintains a pool of reusable connections. Alembic handles database migrations — version-controlled changes to your schema (adding columns, renaming tables) that can be applied and rolled back. For complex queries, raw SQL via session.execute(text("SELECT * FROM products WHERE price > :min_price"), {"min_price": 100}) can be more performant and readable than ORM queries. Use raw SQL for reports and aggregations; use ORM for CRUD operations. Always use parameterized queries (:param), never string formatting with user input — SQL injection is still a real threat.

sections.title: N+1 query problem — ORM's hidden performance killer
sections.context: The N+1 problem: querying a list of authors (1 query) then accessing each author's books (N queries) — 21 total queries for 20 authors. Wrong: for author in session.query(Author): print(author.books). Each loop triggers a new query. Fix use eager loading: session.query(Author).options(joinedload(Author.books)).all() — one JOIN query fetches everything. SQLAlchemy's joinedload or selectinload are your tools. Always check the actual SQL being generated with echo=True or logging.

sections.title: Your turn
sections.prompt: Create an SQLite database for a library system. Define two tables using SQLAlchemy ORM: Author (id, name, birth_year) and Book (id, title, year, author_id as foreign key). Insert 3 authors and 2 books per author (use session.add_all()). Then write a query that fetches all authors born after 1970 along with their books, using eager loading to avoid N+1. Print the results.
hints: How do you define a foreign key relationship in SQLAlchemy using relationship()?
What loading strategy (joinedload or selectinload) prevents the N+1 problem?
